coredata.dc:title: A new journey of fluorescent carbon dots: A shining star in the realm of nucleic acid dyes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Yu
Zhao, Bin
Yan, Guozhen
Zhou, Ting
Xu, Zhihua
Niu, Zhihan
Xu, Zhenghong
Zhang, Tongtong
Shi, Feng
coredata.dc:description: Nucleic acid dyes play important roles in the quantification and detection of nucleic acids by binding to nucleic acids. However, traditional nucleic acid dyes pose great environmental and economic challenges due to their high price, high toxicity, long dyeing time, low sensitivity and insufficient stability. To overcome these problems, we developed a new nucleic acid dye based on carbon dots, which were generated through hydrothermal synthesis with liquorice components as precursors and a system containing ethylenediamine. This new type of carbon dot can effectively replace traditional dyes and can be used to efficiently visualize DNA, RNA and plasmids via agarose gel electrophoresis. Then, we studied the interaction mechanism between carbon dots and nucleic acids by UV–visible spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and circular dichroism spectroscopy. The interaction between the carbon dots and nucleic acids mainly occurred through groove binding and was accompanied by a slight electrostatic interaction. Carbon dots-based nucleic acid dyes, with their low cost, simple synthesis process and high stability, have opened a new path for the field of nucleic acid detection. Its research and development not only promote the frontier progress of biological science and medicine but also introduces innovative strategies and tools for scientific research and clinical practice.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Licorice
Ethylenediamine
Carbon Dots
EB
Nucleic Acid Dyes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and spectral characterization of L-CDs
Binding mode of L-CDs to nucleic acid
Detection of different products by L-CDs and its effect on electrophoretic mobility
Comparison of different nucleic acid dyes
Exploring the binding mode of L-CDs and nucleic acids
Concentration and sensitivity detection of carbon dots as nucleic acid dyes
Carbon dots as nucleic acid dyes for visual detection of DNA, RNA and plasmids

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Transmission electron microscopy results of L-CDs (the upper right illustration is a high-resolution transmission electron microscope of L-CDs; the central illustration is the histogram of L-CDs particle size distribution; the lower right illustration is the structure magnification diagram of L-CDs).

(a) is the UV–vis spectrum and the best excitation/emission spectrum of L-CDs, and the illustration is the diagram of L-CDs under sunlight (left) and ultraviolet light (right) irradiation. (b) is the emission spectrum of L-CDs measured at different excitation wavelengths.
FT-IR and XPS spectra of L-CDs. (a) FT-IR of L-CDs; (b) XPS spectra of L-CDs; (c-e) is the high-resolution spectra of C1s, N1s and O1s of L-CDs.

(a) Shows the UV–Vis spectra of L-CDs-ctDNA complex (The concentrations of L-CDs were 0.015, 0.025, 0.035, 0.045, 0.055, 0.065, 0.075, 0.085, 0.095, and 0.105 mg/mL, and the concentration of ctDNA was 0.025 mg/mL.);(b) The difference spectra of the absorption values of L-CDs-ctDNA complex and L-CDs + ctDNA at 260 nm.
Fluorescence spectra of the interaction between L-CDs and ctDNA (MG).
The CD spectra of L-CDs interacting with ctDNA at different mixing ratios.

(a) The sensitivity of L-CDs as a nucleic acid dye; (b) is the detection effect of L-CDs as nucleic acid dyes on DNA Marker III with different loading amounts; (c) Detection of DNA, RNA and plasmid for L-CDs as nucleic acid dye；(d)The effect of different concentrations of carbon dots on electrophoretic mobility; (e) Comparison of L-CDs as a nucleic acid dye with EB and SYBR for the detection of DNA Marker III (three types of dyes were tested on different gels).
The effects of L-CDs, EB and SYBR on the viability of L929 cells.
Comparison table of carbon dots and other traditional nucleic acid dyes.

row.entry: High security, suitable for conventional laboratory
High security, sensitivity close to EB
Compared with EB, its toxicity is lower, but it still needs to be protected.
High safety, agarose gel experiment can be developed into a conventional experiment (no specific laboratory division), sensitivity close to EB, low cost and easy preparation.

head.ce:title: A new journey of fluorescent carbon dots: A shining star in the realm of nucleic acid dyes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Science, Shihezi University, Xinjiang 832003, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life Science, Shihezi University, Xinjiang 832003, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Xinjiang Plant Medicine Resources Utilization, Ministry of Education 832003, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Xinjiang Plant Medicine Resources Utilization

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Xinjiang Plant Medicine Resources Utilization, Ministry of Education 832003, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nucleic acid dyes play important roles in the quantification and detection of nucleic acids by binding to nucleic acids. However, traditional nucleic acid dyes pose great environmental and economic challenges due to their high price, high toxicity, long dyeing time, low sensitivity and insufficient stability. To overcome these problems, we developed a new nucleic acid dye based on carbon dots, which were generated through hydrothermal synthesis with liquorice components as precursors and a system containing ethylenediamine. This new type of carbon dot can effectively replace traditional dyes and can be used to efficiently visualize DNA, RNA and plasmids via agarose gel electrophoresis. Then, we studied the interaction mechanism between carbon dots and nucleic acids by UV–visible spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and circular dichroism spectroscopy. The interaction between the carbon dots and nucleic acids mainly occurred through groove binding and was accompanied by a slight electrostatic interaction. Carbon dots-based nucleic acid dyes, with their low cost, simple synthesis process and high stability, have opened a new path for the field of nucleic acid detection. Its research and development not only promote the frontier progress of biological science and medicine but also introduces innovative strategies and tools for scientific research and clinical practice.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nucleic acid is one of the most important biological macromolecules, which is the material basis for the storage, transmission and expression of genetic material. Nucleic acid dyes usually stain one or more nucleic acid bases, and the binding between dyes and nucleic acids is mainly accomplished by charge interactions, hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions. When a nucleic acid is negatively charged and the nucleic acid dye is positively charged, binding generally occurs through electrostatic attraction; some groups on the dye can also form hydrogen bonds with nucleic acid bases, thereby binding to nucleic acids [1]. In addition, dyes with strong hydrophobicity can interact with bases that exhibit strong hydrophobicity in nucleic acids. This binding leads to changes in the fluorescent properties of the dye, which are used to quantify and characterize nucleic acids [2–4]. In addition to scientific research, nucleic acid dyes can be applied to some industrial processes and applications in daily life. The fluorescent dye used in nucleic acid detection is generally not carcinogenic, but it can cause irritation. In addition, nucleic acid dyes play an important role in biochemistry, molecular biology, genetics and other fields. Their application not only improves the sensitivity and accuracy of experiments but also promotes the development and progress of related fields. In the field of molecular biology, ethidium bromide (EB) is an extremely important nucleic acid dye, which provides key assistance for many nucleic acid research experiments, but its safety issues are like the sword of Damocles, which has attracted much attention from the academic community. Ethidium bromide has unique physical and chemical properties. It can be accurately embedded between the base pairs of double-stranded DNA and RNA in a non-covalent manner. The main force to maintain the two is the van der Waals force. In a special high ionic strength saturated solution environment, the embedding rule is clear and observable. About every 2.5 bases, an ethidium bromide molecule is successfully inserted. Specifically, when combined with double-stranded DNA, the fluorescence intensity can be increased by 20–30 times; when combined with double-stranded RNA, the fluorescence intensity will increase by as much as 21 times. Even if the nucleic acid content in the gel is minimal, researchers can also use the fluorescence signal emitted by the combination of the two to accurately capture the trace of nucleic acid and achieve efficient nucleic acid detection, which greatly promotes the development of gel electrophoresis, nucleic acid qualitative and quantitative analysis and other experiments. However, the other side of the coin is that ethidium bromide is also a strong mutagen, and the potential carcinogenic risk is always lingering like a hidden threat in the dark. The safety of ethidium bromide has attracted more and more attention from the scientific community, regulatory authorities and experimental personnel. In the whole process of use, storage and disposal, strict norms and standards need to be followed to prevent incalculable damage to human health and ecological environment. See (Table 1 ).

Although EB has excellent nucleic acid binding ability and fluorescence tracing effect, it makes nucleic acid detection and quantitative analysis intuitive and feasible. Its strong mutagenesis and potential carcinogenic risk have become a thorny problem on the road of scientific research and application. The laboratory needs to invest a lot of energy to strictly abide by safety norms, and a little carelessness may endanger the health of operators and pollute the environment. Clinical testing is even more concerned about the safety of patient samples, cannot tolerate a little sloppy. In the face of such difficulties, researchers urgently need new material that can replicate the advantages of EB detection and abandon its toxic shortcomings. The scientific community has never stopped exploring safer and more efficient alternatives to nucleic acid dyes, and the emergence of carbon dots (CDs), just like a dawn, dispels haze and brings new hope.

CDs are widely used in current scientific research. They are carbon materials with a particle size of less than 10 nm in at least one dimension, close to quasi-spherical in micromorphology, and fluorescent properties. The prepared fluorescent carbon materials are usually composed of sp2/sp3 carbon, oxygen, nitrogen groups and modified chemical groups. As emerging nanomaterials, they show remarkable characteristics and potential. The emergence of CDs has led to new opportunities and breakthroughs for many disciplines, such as materials science, chemistry, and biology. CDs exhibit unique optical, electrical and chemical properties and are small but contain a large amount of energy and potential. Due to their excellent fluorescence properties, these materials have broad application prospects in the fields of biological imaging, sensing and detection [5–10]. Moreover, CDs exhibit good biocompatibility and low toxicity, which lays an important foundation for their in-depth application in the biomedical field [11–13]. With further research, the methods used to prepare CDs have also been increasingly enriched and improved. Scientists are committed to obtaining carbon dots with better performance and more accurate structures through various innovative approaches. The continuous exploration of the properties and applications of CDs not only expands our knowledge of nanomaterials but also promotes the development and progress of related technologies. Thus, CDs have become a hot topic in today's scientific community.

Licorice (Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch.) roots and rhizomes are commonly used in traditional Chinese medicine for their detoxification, anti-inflammatory, antitussive, anti-tumour, anti-ulcer, antibacterial activities, etc [14]. The dry root of licorice contains aromatic substances and is processed into licorice paste after being crushed, which is a water-soluble colloidal substance and can be used as a raw material for food, beverage and tobacco flavours. Licorice has been recorded in Chinese herbal books in past dynasties and is known as the king of all medicines. The material exerts a certain effect on the treatment of chronic bronchitis and asthmatic bronchitis.

Ethylenediamine (EDA), also known as 1,2-diaminoethane, is a typical aliphatic diamine [15]. It has a wide range of applications in many fields, including fuel manufacturing, rubber vulcanization accelerator production, drug synthesis, and as a solvent and emulsifier, such as fibrin. In addition, EDA is used as an intermediate for products such as epoxy resin curing agents and insulating paint coatings. The chemical properties of EDA are highly dependent on the characteristics of the primary amines and secondary amines in its structure. Its chemical properties are affected by the degree of substitution of hydrogen atoms on the –NH2 EDA is easily soluble in water and exothermic during the dissolution process. Its aqueous solution is strongly alkaline; however, its alkaline solution is weaker than that of aliphatic amines but stronger than that of ammonia. As an important raw material and intermediate or final product of many drugs, EDA plays an important role in the field of medicine; in addition, EDA plays an indispensable role in the ecological circulatory system [16]. Therefore, EDA is an important pharmaceutical excipient for the smooth muscle relaxant aminophylline, which is widely used to treat respiratory diseases, such as asthma.

At present, the research on licorice-based carbon dots has not been reported. Using this as a raw material to synthesize carbon dots can open new research directions and inject new ideas into the field of carbon dot preparation. On the other hand, the application of licorice-based carbon dots in the field of nucleic acid dyes is still blank. Exploring its potential in nucleic acid detection is expected to provide new materials for the development of nucleic acid dyes and promote the development of this technology. In addition, licorice, as a traditional Chinese medicine, has high safety through long-term clinical verification. Compared with commonly used chemical synthetic raw materials, licorice can effectively reduce potential risks in biomedical detection applications. Based on the above content, this study explored the preparation of carbon dots using licorice as a precursor for the first time and successfully applied it to the field of nucleic acid detection for the first time. In this study, we used licorice powder as a biomass precursor to prepare a new type of CDs by hydrothermal method in a system containing EDA. It also shows the potential as a nucleic acid dye for effective visual detection of DNA, RNA and plasmids by agarose gel electrophoresis. The experimental results show that the CDs are fully applicable to this field. In addition, this study provides a new biomass source for the preparation of CDs, provides an efficient and new visualization method for the field of nucleic acid detection, and shows the great potential and application value of licorice components in the field of nanomaterials and biology.

Ural licorice root powder (laboratory autogenous grinding), ethylenediamine and ctDNA were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Company. DNA Marker III, loading buffer, 50x TAE, and DEPC water were purchased from Heng Chao Biotechnology Co., Ltd. DMEM culture medium Sigma, USA; mTT solution Beijing Solarbio Company. Nucleic acid experimental samples were provided by the Key Laboratory of Plant Medicine Resources Utilization of the Ministry of Education in Xinjiang. PCR products, Gossypium hirsutum RNA and chemical reagents were used according to the regulations, and the test water was ultrapure water.

To prepare L-CDs, 1 g of licorice root powder and 300 μL of ethylenediamine were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water and reacted at 180℃ for 6 h in a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined reactor. After the reaction was completed, the mixture was cooled to room temperature. The initial product was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min, and the supernatant was collected for washing and filtration through a microporous membrane (0.22 μm). The obtained suspension was subjected to vacuum freeze-drying to obtain L-CDs powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and spectral characterization of L-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: TEM images were obtained with a Hitachi HT7700 transmission electron microscope. High-resolution transmission electron microscope images were obtained by an FEI Tecnai G2 F20 high-resolution field emission transmission electron microscope. The particle size was measured by Nano Measurement Instrument 1.2 software and a CGS-3 dynamic and static laser light scattering instrument. The UV–visible absorption spectra were measured by a SpectraMax spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) of the sample was performed with a Nicolet iS-10 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer. XPS data were measured by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The zeta potential was detected by a Nano ZS90 potential tester produced by Malvern, UK. The circular dichroism spectral data were measured by an Applied Photophysics Chirascan V100. Gel images were recorded using a gel imaging analyser (Bio-Rad Gel-Doc EZ, USA) and a Canon 850D camera.

We used L-CDs to prepare solutions at concentrations of 0.5 mg/mL, 1 mg/mL, 3 mg/mL, 5 mg/mL and 10 mg/mL, respectively, as the optimal concentration for testing the application of L-CDs in nucleic acid dyes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding mode of L-CDs to nucleic acid
ce:section.ce:para: The concentrations of L-CDs were 0.015 mg/mL, 0.025 mg/mL, 0.035 mg/mL, 0.045 mg/mL, 0.055 mg/mL, 0.065 mg/mL, 0.075 mg/mL, 0.085 mg/mL, 0.095 mg/mL, and 0.105 mg/mL, the concentration of ctDNA was 0.025 mg/mL, and the concentration of MG was 0.0125 mg/mL. The UV–vis spectral data of L-CDs-ctDNA were measured at 220–300 nm after the L-CDs-ctDNA complex was incubated in a constant-temperature water bath at 35 °C for 30 min in a system with a volume ratio of 1:1. The fluorescence spectrum of ctDNA-MG complex in the presence of carbon dots was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 620 nm, and the fluorescence intensity at 665 nm was recorded. FT-IR spectral data in the range of 1800 cm−1-800 cm−1 were measured after L-CDs were added at concentrations of 0.075 mg/mL, 0.150 mg/mL, 0.225 mg/mL and 0.025 mg/mL ctDNA (Tris-HCl, pH 8.0). The CD spectra of the CDs-ctDNA complexes at 220–300 nm were measured at [L-CDs]: [ctDNA] ratios of 0.4:1, 0.8:1 and 1.2:1.

DNA Marker III is a mixture of seven double-stranded linear DNA fragments with an indicator band of 1200 bp, which is easy to observe after electrophoresis. Each band can be used to determine the size and content of the target fragment by strict physical quantification. The concentration of the product was 120 ng/μL. The content of DNA in the non-indicative band was 10 ng/μL, so 0.5 μL, 1 μL, 3 μL, 5 μL and 10 μL of DNA Marker III was added to the sample wells, and the voltage was maintained at 120 V for 20 min. After bromophenol blue electrophoresis was performed to 1–2 cm of the gel, the staining effect of each band could be clearly observed in the automatic gel imaging analyser (Bio-Rad Gel-Doc EZ, USA). This was performed to determine the minimum detection limit of L-CDs as nucleic acid dyes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of different products by L-CDs and its effect on electrophoretic mobility
ce:section.ce:para: L-CDs were used as nucleic acid dyes to detect DNA, RNA and plasmids. To explore the effect of different concentrations of carbon dots on electrophoretic mobility, we selected L-CDs with concentrations of 10 mg / mL, 20 mg / mL and 30 mg / mL to detect DNA samples with a size of 3500 bp. Agarose (0.25 g) was added to 25 mL of 1X TAE and cooled to prepare a 1 % agarose gel. After the 10 mg/mL L-CDs solution was added and mixed well, it was inserted into the comb and allowed to coagulate. After the comb was removed, it was placed in the electrophoresis tank, and the buffer was not passed through the gel. The obtained product was added to the spotting well at a voltage of 120 V for 20 min. After bromophenol blue electrophoresis to 1–2 cm of the gel, the corresponding bands can be clearly seen in the gel imager.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of different nucleic acid dyes
ce:section.ce:para: L-CDs were compared with common nucleic acid dyes EB and SYBR on the market. Agarose (0.25 g) was added to 25 mL 1XTAE, and 1 % agarose gel was prepared after cooling. The same number of L-CDs, EB and SYBR solutions were added respectively. After mixing, it was inserted into the comb to coagulate. After the comb was taken out, it was placed in an electrophoresis tank, and the buffer did not pass through the gel. DNA Marker III was added to the spotting hole as the detection target sample, the voltage was 120 V, and the time was 20 min. After bromophenol blue electrophoresis to 1–2 cm gel, the corresponding bands can be clearly seen on the gel imager.

The cytotoxicity of L-CDs, EB and SYBR dyes on mouse fibroblasts (L929) was evaluated by MTT assay. Firstly, 10 mg of the prepared dye was taken and put into a conical flask containing 30 mL DMEM high glucose culture medium. At 37 °C, the conical flask was placed in a constant temperature oscillator at a speed of 50 rpm for 12 h to obtain a dye suspension. Mouse fibroblasts (L929) in logarithmic growth phase were seeded in cell culture plates at a density of 5 × 103 cells per well, with a volume of 100 μL per well. They were randomly divided into 5 experimental groups, 1 control group and 1 blank group, with 6 replicate wells in each group. The cells were cultured in a 5 % CO2 incubator at 37 °C for 4 h. After the cells adhered well under the microscope, the medium was discarded. L-CDs, EB and SYBR extracts were diluted with medium to 20 μg/mL, 40 μg/mL, 60 μg/mL, 80 μg/mL and 100 μg/mL, respectively. 100 μL was added to each of the three groups of experimental holes, and 100 μL of normal DMEM high glucose medium was added to the control group. The plates were placed in a constant temperature incubator for 48 h and then removed. 10 μL of CCK-8 solution was added to each well and then incubated for 2 h. Finally, the absorbance was measured at 450 nm using an enzyme-linked immunosorbent detector. The data were recorded, and the relative cell proliferation rate was calculated to determine the cytotoxicity of the three dyes.

In this study, to develop new nucleic acid dye, we first synthesized L-CDs and characterized the L-CDs through various methods. L-CDs were used for subsequent experiments. As observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), the morphology of the L-CDs was close to spherical and uniformly dispersed. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the synthesized L-CDs showed obvious lattice fringes. The lattice spacing is 0.21 nm, which is similar to the lattice fringes of graphene (100), further indicating that L-CDs were successfully prepared. The size of the blue fluorescence-emitting L-CDs particles was measured to be 1.71 ± 0.31 nm (Fig. 1 ).

Ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis) is an electronic spectrum that is produced by the transition of valence electrons [17]. It is an important method for studying the absorption of materials. The composition, content and structure of the substance can be analyzed, determined and inferred by the ultraviolet–visible spectrum and absorption degree generated by the absorption of ultraviolet and visible light by the molecules or ions of the substance [18]. UV spectroscopy can be used to deduce whether the molecular skeleton of organic compounds contains conjugated structures, such as C=C-C=C, C=C-C=O, and benzene rings. Nitrogen, oxygen and water vapour in the air absorb the spectrum within the far ultraviolet region of 100 ∼ 200 nm. In the near-UV region of 200 ∼ 400 nm, mainly aromatic compounds or conjugated compounds absorb light energy. L-CDs exhibit strong absorption in the UV region of 250 ∼ 300 nm, which is mainly due to their large sp2 conjugated structure and abundant surface functional groups, which may contain simple non-conjugated and n-electron chromophore groups, such as carbonyl (C=O) or other related groups generated by n-π*transitions (Fig. 2 a). The excitation wavelength (Ex) and emission wavelength (Em) of L-CDs were 420 nm and 496 nm, respectively, when the excitation wavelength is 420 nm, the fluorescence intensity of L-CDs is the strongest, that is, 420 nm is selected as the excitation wavelength, and the quantum yield of the prepared L-CDs is determined by Edinburgh-steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer FLS1000, which is 12.05 %. Notably, L-CDs exhibit a slight excitation wavelength dependence; that is, the emission wavelength shifts slightly with changing excitation wavelength (Fig. 2 b). This characteristic may be related to the size and surface state of the L-CDs [19]. The different sizes of L-CDs affect their band gap, and the surface state is similar to the molecular state, which leads to the complexity of the excited state of L-CDs. In addition, the size effect results from the quantum confinement effect, which further enhances the diversity of L-CDs emission characteristics [20].

Compared to infrared spectroscopy, the identification of organic compounds by ultraviolet spectroscopy is far less effective because many compounds have no absorption or only weak absorption in the ultraviolet region, the ultraviolet spectrum is generally simple, and their characteristics are weak. As shown in Fig. 3 , the structure of L-CDs was analyses by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) [21] and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) [22]. In the FT-IR spectrum, we found that L-CDs have multiple characteristic emission peaks, which correspond to different vibration modes. Specifically, the emission peak near 1047 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of C-N, and the peak near 1388 cm -1 reflects the C-H bending or deformation vibration of methyl and methylene groups. The peak at 1647 cm−1 is related to the N-H bending vibration of the amid and the C=C skeleton vibration of the olefin. In addition, the emission peak near 2143 cm−1 is attributed to the C≡C bending vibration of alkynes. The emission peak near 2953 cm−1 indicates that there is a C-H stretching vibration of saturated hydrocarbons in L-CDs. The N-H stretching vibrations of amino acids and amides and the –OH stretching vibrations of alcohols and phenols are reflected near 3359 cm−1 (Fig. 3 a). To further verify the FT-IR spectroscopy results, XPS analysis was performed. Fig. 3 (b) is the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy of L-CDs. It can be clearly seen from the figure that the synthesized powder has a large amount of C, N, O, and elements, and the measured contents are 65.12 %, 21.86 %, and 13.02 %, respectively, which lays a foundation for the subsequent application of L-CDs in nucleic acid dyes. The deconvolution of C1s, O1s and N1s peaks in Fig. 3 (b) can also be used to analyze the surface functional groups of L-CDs. For example, Fig. 3 (c-e) can be fitted to obtain multiple peaks, namely C-C/C =C/C-H (284.80 eV), C-OH (286.94 eV), C = O (532.09 eV), C-O (533.44 eV), N-N (402.92 eV), C-N / N-O (399.49 eV), etc. The results of XPS analysis are consistent with the results of FT-IR, which once again proves the existence of various functional groups of L-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploring the binding mode of L-CDs and nucleic acids
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the complex interaction mechanism between small molecules and DNA, we used a variety of spectroscopy methods, such as ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy, for detailed analysis. There are usually three binding modes between L-CDs and nucleic acids: electrostatic interactions, groove interactions and intercalation [23]. First, electrostatic interactions are nonselective interactions based on the charge attraction between positively charged small molecules and negatively charged ribose-phosphate backbones of nucleic acid molecules. Due to the negative charge of nucleic acids, the L-CDs we studied had a positive charge of + 3.68 mV on their surface. Even if the charge density is low, it can promote electrostatic interactions between L-CDs and nucleic acid molecules. Second, in the groove binding mode, small molecule compounds selectively interact with the base pair edge of the large groove or small groove of the DNA molecule. This mode of action can effectively bind DNA without embedding the DNA structure and prevent it from unwinding. Finally, the intercalation occurs via an aromatic ring molecular system with a planar structure or an approximately planar structure, which is directly inserted between the base pairs inside the DNA double helix. This intercalative binding originates from the π-π conjugated system formed between the aromatic ring and the base pair, which not only achieves electrical stability but also plays a hydrophobic role [24].

To reveal the binding mode of L-CDs and nucleic acids more accurately, we measured the UV–vis, FT-IR and CD spectra [25,26] of L-CDs and calf thymus DNA (ctDNA) complexes. This spectral data will provide insights into the mechanism underlying the interaction between L-CDs and nucleic acids, which will help us fully understand this complex and delicate biomolecular interaction process. UV–visible absorption spectroscopy is a powerful tool for studying interactions between small molecules and DNA because interactions between molecules usually lead to specific changes in the absorption spectrum. By comparing the absorption spectra (Fig. 4 a) and differential spectra (Fig. 4 b) of different concentrations of L-CDs and ctDNA, we can reveal the molecular mechanism behind these changes. With increasing L-CDs concentration, we observed that the absorbance of the L-CDs-ctDNA complex at 260 nm was significantly greater than the sum of the absorbance of the L-CDs and ctDNA. The hyperchromic effect is more obvious, while the position of the absorption peak remains basically unchanged. The hyperchromic effect usually indicates a non-intercalated interaction mode because the intercalating effect often leads to a significant shift in the spectrum [27]. As the NH2 group in the L-CDs structure can be used as a hydrogen bond donor, it may form hydrogen bonds with purines and pyrimidines in ctDNA, which are potential hydrogen bond acceptors. This non-covalent interaction provides a reasonable explanation for the hyperchromic effect [28]. Based on the above observations and analysis, we can infer that the interaction between L-CDs and ctDNA is likely dominated by groove or electrostatic forces rather than intercalation. This discovery not only deepens our understanding of the interaction mechanism between L-CDs and DNA but also provides a new perspective and strong support for the application potential of L-CDs in the field of nucleic acid dyes.

To further understand the binding mode of carbon dots and ctDNA, we used methyl green (MG) as a fluorescent probe to study the competitive binding of carbon dots to ctDNA-MG system. MG is a typical DNA groove binding probe, which can be used as a spectral probe to study the binding mode of material molecules to DNA. If the material molecules bind to the groove region of DNA, the carbon dots can compete for the groove binding sites of MG and DNA, so that ctDNA-MG can be complexed. The fluorescence intensity of the complex was greatly reduced. As shown in Fig. 5 , the fluorescence intensity of MG increased significantly after binding to DNA. However, after the addition of carbon dots, the fluorescence intensity of the ctDNA-MG complex gradually decreased with the increase of the concentration of carbon dots, indicating that carbon dots replaced some MG molecules and dissociated MG molecules from the ctDNA-MG system.

Circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy is an advanced analytical technique used to explore the conformational changes produced by interactions between material molecules and DNA [30]. In the CD spectrum, the characteristic peaks of DNA provide important information about its structure. The negative peak at 245 nm is a sign of the double helix structure of DNA, while the positive peak at 275 nm reflects the base accumulation state [31]. These characteristic peaks reveal subtle differences in the molecular structure of DNA. When DNA interacts with small molecules, the change in the CD spectrum can indicate the type and degree of interaction. In particular, the strong interaction of intercalative binding significantly affects the structure of DNA, which is reflected in the CD spectrum as a significant change in the position or intensity of the positive and negative peaks [32]. In our study, we added L-CDs to ctDNA and monitored the changes in CD spectra. As shown in Fig. 6, the CD spectra of ctDNA did not significantly change after the L-CDs were added. This result indicates that the interaction between L-CDs and ctDNA involves mainly groove binding rather than intercalative binding, so it does not have a significant effect on the right-handed B-helix structure or base accumulation of ctDNA. Based on these findings, we can infer that the interaction between L-CDs and ctDNA does not markedly change the conformational properties of ctDNA. This conclusion not only provides an important basis for obtaining in-depth knowledge on the interaction mechanism between L-CDs and ctDNA but also provides new insights and strong support for the potential of L-CDs in biotechnology applications, such as nucleic acid dyes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Concentration and sensitivity detection of carbon dots as nucleic acid dyes
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the optimal concentration of L-CDs in nucleic acid dyes, we conducted agarose gel electrophoresis experiments to evaluate the binding effect of different concentrations of L-CDs with nucleic acids. The results showed that when the concentration of L-CDs was 10 mg/mL or 100 mg/mL, the strip clarity and sensitivity of electrophoresis were almost identical (Fig. 8 a). Considering economic factors and efficiency, we chose 10 mg/mL as the concentration of L-CDs for subsequent electrophoresis experiments. In this study, we used DNA Marker III, a mixture of seven double-stranded linear DNA fragments with an indicator band of 1200 bp. Through precise physical quantification, these bands can be used to accurately determine the size and content of the target DNA fragment. We loaded different amounts of DNA Marker III into the gel and performed electrophoresis at 120 V for 20 min until the bromophenol blue had migrated to 1–2 cm of the gel. Subsequently, in the automatic gel imaging analyzer, we observed the clear staining effect of the L-CDs on DNA. The results showed that the prepared L-CDs could detect 10 ng/μL of DNA standard in 1 % agarose gel electrophoresis, which was comparable to the sensitivity of the traditional nucleic acid dye EB (Fig. 8b). Further analysis of the results of gel electrophoresis showed that L-CDs had little effect on the mobility of nucleic acids, which was in stark contrast to the significant effect of intercalative binding on DNA mobility. This finding further confirmed that the binding mode of L-CDs to DNA involved groove binding rather than intercalative binding. This finding not only provides a basis for us to choose the appropriate concentration of L-CDs but also provides an important clue for us to understand the interaction mechanism between L-CDs and DNA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dots as nucleic acid dyes for visual detection of DNA, RNA and plasmids
ce:section.ce:para: To ensure that the L-CDs can be successfully applied as nucleic acid dyes, we also performed agarose gel electrophoresis with DNA, RNA and plasmids. Surprisingly, the prepared L-CDs could clearly detect DNA, RNA, and plasmids, and the band size and clarity corresponded with expectations (Fig. 8c). To explore the effect of different concentrations of carbon dots on electrophoretic mobility, we selected L-CDs with concentrations of 10 mg/mL, 20 mg/mL and 30 mg/mL to test DNA samples with a size of 3500 bp. From the experimental results, the concentration of L-CDs, whether they are 10 mg/mL, 20 mg/mL or 30 mg/mL, has little effect on electrophoretic mobility (Fig. 8d). From the comparative experimental results, the detection effect of L-CDs as nucleic acid dyes on DNA Marker III is no less than that of EB but better than that of SYBR (Fig. 8e). This shows that the L-CDs prepared in this experiment have basic conditions such as nucleic acid dye. By comparing EB and SYBR, L-CDs have shown unique advantages in safety, stability and nucleic acid binding specificity. In summary, L-CDs can bind to DNA, RNA and plasmids, and have the basic conditions for application in the field of nucleic acid dyes. These experimental results not only prove the high sensitivity of L-CDs to nucleic acid detection but also verify its feasibility as a nucleic acid dye. It provides a new nucleic acid dye for future molecular and biochemical experiments and lays a solid foundation for its wide application in nucleic acid detection, gene diagnosis, biomedical research and other fields. It is expected to become a new, efficient and safe alternative to nucleic acid dyes and promote technological development and progress in related fields.

MTT assay was used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the three dyes on L929 cells. The cytotoxicity of different dyes at different concentrations on L929 cells is shown in Fig. 9 . According to the calculated relative cell proliferation rate, when the experimental group was diluted to 100 μg/mL, the average cell proliferation rates of L-CDs, EB and SYBR were 85.31 %, 36.26 % and 76.34 %, respectively, which were 7.95 %, 57 % and 16.92 %, respectively, compared with 93.26 % in the control group. It can be seen that 100 μg/mL L-CDs and SYBR showed slight cytotoxicity to mouse fibroblasts, while EB showed obvious cytotoxicity to mouse fibroblasts; when diluted to 80 μg/mL, the average cell proliferation rates of L-CDs, EB and SYBR were 87.19 %, 39.15 % and 81.26 %, respectively, which were 5.67 %, 53.71 % and 11.6 % lower than that of the control group (92.86 %); When diluted to 60 μg/mL, the average cell proliferation rates of L-CDs, EB and SYBR were 89.14 %, 41.26 % and 84.54 %, respectively. Among them, L-CDs were slightly lower than the control group 93.13 %, with a difference of only 3.99 %. At this time, the cytotoxicity to cells has been reduced to a very low level; when diluted to 40 μg/mL, the average cell proliferation rates of L-CDs, EB and SYBR were 91.29 %, 42.61 % and 86.36 %, respectively, which were 1.9 %, 50.58 % and 6.83 % lower than that of the control group (93.19 %). When diluted to 20 μg/mL, the average proliferation rates of L-CDs, EB and SYBR cells increased to 92.39 %, 43.86 % and 89.56 %, respectively. The proliferation rate of the control group with normal DMEM high glucose medium was 93.3 %. At this time, the cytotoxicity of L-CDs compared with EB was minimal (Fig. 9).

Finally, it can be concluded that at the concentration of 100 μg/mL or 80 μg/mL, L-CDs and SYBR extracts showed mild cytotoxicity to cells, which would slightly affect cell growth, while EB showed more obvious cytotoxicity to cells; at 40 μg/mL, the cytotoxicity of L-CDs has been reduced to a negligible level. According to the cytotoxicity evaluation grade in Table 2 , L-CDs had no obvious cytotoxicity to L929 cells at low concentration (20 μg/mL), and the cytotoxicity grade was 0. At the medium concentration of 40 μg/mL and 60 μg/mL, the cell proliferation rate was 89.14 %-91.29 %, and the cytotoxicity grade was 0; at high concentrations of 80 μg/mL and 100 μg/mL, the proliferation rate of L929 cells decreased slightly to 87.19 %-85.31 %, and there was no cytotoxicity to L929 cells. The cytotoxicity grade of 100 μg/mL L-CDs was grade 1. Therefore, the L-CDs prepared in this experiment showed extremely slight cytotoxicity to cells and could be applied in subsequent experiments.

In this study, L-CDs with blue fluorescence emission characteristics were successfully prepared by using licorice as precursor. The purpose of this study is to create a new type of nano nucleic acid dye to replace the traditional chemical reagent dye. To clarify the interaction mechanism between L-CDs and ctDNA, we used a variety of spectroscopic methods, such as UV–vis, FT-IR, FL and CD spectroscopy, for detailed study. First, through UV–vis spectroscopy analysis, we observed that with increasing L-CDs concentration, the absorption of L-CDs-ctDNA at 260 nm was much greater than that of L-CDs and ctDNA, and the absorption peak position remained basically unchanged. This hyperchromic effect and lack of redshift strongly indicate that the interaction between L-CDs and ctDNA occurs mainly through the groove binding mode. In addition, since the L-CDs structure contains positively charged electrophilic nitrogen atoms and the nucleic acid has a negative charge, the electrostatic interaction between the two also provides the possibility for this binding mode. In the fluorescence spectrum, methyl green (MG) was used as a groove binding probe, and the interaction mechanism of CDs was revealed by competitive binding experiments. The results showed that CDs could competitively replace MG molecules in ctDNA-MG complexes, resulting in a significant decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the system with the increase of CDs concentration (Fig. 5). Furthermore, FT-IR spectroscopy revealed more specific interactions between the L-CDs and ctDNA. Although the positions of the A and T base stretching vibration peaks of ctDNA did not change with increasing L-CDs concentration, the stretching vibration bands of the C and G bases red-shifted, which clearly indicated that there was a strong interaction between the L-CDs and the G-C base pairs of ctDNA. CD spectroscopic analysis further confirmed the above findings, verifying that these four L-CDs bind to the grooves of ctDNA and did not significantly affect the right-handed B-helix or base accumulation of ctDNA; that is, the binding of L-CDs did not change the conformational properties of ctDNA. In practical applications, this new type of L-CDs showed excellent performance in 1 % agarose gel electrophoresis and could effectively bind to DNA, RNA and plasmids. Considering that most fluorescent dyes stain single-stranded DNA or RNA by binding to the molecule to form a shorter in-chain helix, we infer that the binding mode of L-CDs to ctDNA is mainly groove binding and may be accompanied by a slight electrostatic force. Notably, the sensitivity of the L-CDs at a concentration of 10 mg/mL for nucleic acid detection in this study reached 10 ng/μL, which is comparable to that of the traditional nucleic acid dye EB. However, L-CDs have shown significant advantages in chemical inertness, photobleaching resistance, cost, and ease of preparation, indicating that L-CDs have broad application prospects in the field of nucleic acid dyes. The emergence of this new type of CDs not only provides a more diversified choice of nucleic acid dyes for molecular and biochemical experiments but is also expected to promote the further development of molecular and biochemical experimental techniques [33].

This study focused on the interaction mode between L-CDs and DNA molecules and preliminarily confirmed that they interact mainly through groove binding. However, assessing the safety of L-CDs is a complex and comprehensive process that cannot be limited by the research results. To ensure the safe use of L-CDs in biological applications, we need to conduct detailed and multi-angle in-depth research at the molecular level. This includes experiments on the interaction mechanism between L-CDs and nucleic acids, as it is necessary to explore how L-CDs interact with DNA and RNA and how these interactions affect the structure and function of nucleic acids. In addition, interactions between L-CDs and proteins should be explored, as proteins are important molecules in organisms and understanding the interaction between L-CDs and proteins is essential for assessing their biosafety. Lastly, the cleavage of plasmid DNA by L-CDs should be studied. As plasmid DNA plays an important role in genetic engineering and gene therapy, it is highly important to study whether L-CDs have a potential cleavage effect and to evaluate their biosafety. Through these comprehensive and detailed studies, we can more accurately evaluate the biosafety of L-CDs and provide a more reliable basis for their wide application in the field of nucleic acid dyes and other biological applications. Although some preliminary research results have been completed in this paper, further exploration is still needed to ensure that L- CDs can be applied safely and effectively in the field of nucleic acid dyes.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Project No:32060224), Shihezi University innovation and development special fund (Project No: CXFZ202110) and Municipal Finance Science and Technology Plan Project (Project No:2024YL04).

Yu Ma: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Bin Zhao: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Guozhen Yan: Visualization, Methodology. Ting Zhou: Visualization. Zhihua Xu: Validation. Zhihan Niu: Validation. Zhenghong Xu: Validation. Tongtong Zhang: Validation. Feng Shi: Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: F.S. acknowledgments support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32060224); the Innovative Development Project of Shihezi University (CXFZ202110) and Municipal Finance Science and Technology Plan Project (Project No:2024YL04).
